does: This position paper argues that post-hoc mitigation techniques like machine unlearning and inference-time guardrails cannot retroactively cure legal liability for unauthorized training data acquisition in generative AI systems. The authors develop a three-part legal argument: first, that unauthorized copying during training constitutes a completed infringement act where model weights function as persistent copies retaining training-derived expressive value, rendering later filtering legally insufficient; second, that contractual restrictions via licensing and terms of service, combined with tort and unfair-competition principles around free-riding, can restrict data use independently of copyright defenses like fair use or text-and-data-mining exceptions; and third, that since value from protected inputs persists in learned weights, appropriate remedies including unjust enrichment and disgorgement may require stripping model gains or even removing trained models entirely.
connects: This work responds directly to the machine learning community's increasing reliance on post-hoc mitigation as a defense against infringement claims during an era of intensifying legal challenges to generative AI training practices. It builds on established copyright and tort law frameworks while engaging with recent ML proposals for unlearning and inference-time filtering. The paper connects to broader debates about fair use for text-and-data-mining, the nature of machine learning outputs as derivative works, and tensions between copyright exhaustion doctrine and digital artifacts. It also engages with contract law principles governing terms of service and licensing restrictions that have historically governed data access, bringing these established legal doctrines into conversation with emerging ML techniques.
enables: This position paper opens several future research and policy directions. It suggests that technical mitigation alone may be insufficient to resolve AI copyright and compliance disputes, potentially pushing the ML community toward developing training practices that respect data lineage and source restrictions from inception rather than relying on post-hoc fixes. The argument points toward the need for legal frameworks addressing persistent value in learned weights and model contamination, potentially informing policies around model registration, data provenance documentation, and training-time compliance verification. It may also spur work on technical approaches to model lineage tracking and source attribution, as well as economic analyses of remedies that might apply to trained weights themselves. The analysis suggests future litigation and legislation will likely focus on training-time conduct rather than output-time mitigation.
